title: End of module: Projectiles from a Height

questions.text: A projectile is launched from ground level and lands at the same height it was launched from. Ignoring air resistance, which launch angle maximises its range?

A projectile is launched from the top of a cliff (height $h > 0$ above the landing point) rather than from ground level. Compared with the ground-level case using the same speed and angle, the time of flight is…
choices: impossible to say without more information
questions.explanation: Landing time solves $\tfrac12 g t^2 - u_y t - h = 0$, giving $t = \dfrac{u_y + \sqrt{u_y^2 + 2gh}}{g}$. Since $h>0$ makes the square root exceed $u_y$, this is always larger than the ground-level value $\dfrac{2u_y}{g}$.

questions.text: For launches from an elevated point ($h > 0$), how does the launch angle that maximises range compare with the ground-level optimum of $45^\circ$?
questions.explanation: Raising the launch point effectively gives extra 'free' flight time from the fall, so keeping more speed directed horizontally (a flatter, smaller angle) increases range further. The optimal angle for $h>0$ is always below $45^\circ$, decreasing as $h$ grows relative to $u^2/g$.

questions.text: A ball is launched horizontally (at $\theta = 0^\circ$) from a height $h$ above the ground. Which statement about its motion is correct?
choices: Its vertical motion is identical to an object simply dropped from height $h$
It never reaches the ground because $u_y = 0$
Its horizontal speed decreases as it falls
questions.explanation: With $\theta=0$, $u_y=0$, so the vertical equation $h = \tfrac12 g t^2$ is exactly the free-fall equation for an object dropped from rest — horizontal speed does not affect vertical motion.

questions.text: A ball is launched horizontally at $u = 15\,\text{m s}^{-1}$ from a platform $h = 25\,\text{m}$ above the ground ($g = 9.81\,\text{m s}^{-2}$). Find the time of flight.
questions.explanation: Horizontal launch means $u_y = 0$, so $h = \tfrac12 g t^2$ gives $t = \sqrt{\dfrac{2h}{g}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\times 25}{9.81}} = \sqrt{5.097} = 2.258\,\text{s}$.

questions.text: Using the same ball as the previous question ($u = 15\,\text{m s}^{-1}$ horizontal, $h = 25\,\text{m}$, $t = 2.258\,\text{s}$), find its horizontal range.
questions.explanation: Horizontal speed is constant at $u_x = u = 15\,\text{m s}^{-1}$, so $R = u\,t = 15 \times 2.258 = 33.9\,\text{m}$.

questions.text: A projectile is launched at $u = 20\,\text{m s}^{-1}$, $\theta = 20^\circ$, from the top of a $30\,\text{m}$ cliff ($g = 9.81\,\text{m s}^{-2}$). Find the time of flight.
For the same cliff launch ($u = 20\,\text{m s}^{-1}$, $\theta = 20^\circ$, $h = 30\,\text{m}$, $t = 3.267\,\text{s}$), find the range.
questions.explanation: $R = u_x t = 18.79 \times 3.267 = 61.4\,\text{m}$, using the horizontal component found in the previous question.

questions.text: A projectile is launched at $u = 16\,\text{m s}^{-1}$, $\theta = 50^\circ$, from a platform $h = 10\,\text{m}$ above the ground ($g = 9.81\,\text{m s}^{-2}$). Find its maximum height above the ground.
questions.explanation: $u_y = 16\sin50^\circ = 12.26\,\text{m s}^{-1}$. Extra height above launch: $\dfrac{u_y^2}{2g} = \dfrac{12.26^2}{19.62} = 7.66\,\text{m}$. Adding the platform height: $H_{\text{above ground}} = 10 + 7.66 = 17.66\,\text{m}$.

questions.text: A projectile is launched at $u = 20\,\text{m s}^{-1}$ (at any angle) from a height $h = 25\,\text{m}$ above the ground ($g = 9.81\,\text{m s}^{-2}$). Find its speed on landing.
questions.explanation: Landing speed depends only on $u$ and $h$, not the angle: $v_{\text{land}} = \sqrt{u^2 + 2gh} = \sqrt{20^2 + 2\times9.81\times25} = \sqrt{400 + 490.5} = \sqrt{890.5} = 29.84\,\text{m s}^{-1}$.
